items.html:
What causes food poisoning

Food poisoning is illness caused by eating contaminated food. Most cases come from harmful bacteria such as Salmonella, Campylobacter, E. coli and Listeria, or from viruses like norovirus. These germs are usually invisible, and food that carries them can look, smell and taste perfectly normal.

Harmful bacteria reach food through poor hygiene, cross-contamination from raw meat, undercooking, or letting cooked food sit in the warm danger zone where bacteria multiply. Symptoms often appear hours or days later and include nausea, being sick, diarrhoea, stomach cramps and a raised temperature.

High-risk foods and vulnerable people

Some foods support the growth of bacteria more readily and need extra care. These high-risk foods are usually ready to eat and moist:

  • Cooked meat, poultry and gravy: handle, cool and store these promptly.
  • Dairy, eggs and cooked rice: keep them chilled and use within their date.
  • Shellfish, pâté and prepared salads: treat as ready-to-eat and keep them cold.

Many people you support are more vulnerable to serious illness — older adults, those who are frail or unwell, and anyone with a weakened immune system. What gives you a mild upset stomach could put them in hospital, so careful food handling protects lives.

items.question: Why do care workers need to take extra care with food safety for the older and unwell people they support?

answers.text: Vulnerable people are more likely to become seriously ill from food poisoning that might only mildly affect a healthy adult.
answers.feedback: Correct. Older, frail and immunocompromised people can be much more severely affected, so good food hygiene genuinely protects their health.

answers.text: Older people always dislike the taste of fresh food.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. This is not true and has nothing to do with food safety; the real reason is their greater vulnerability to illness.

answers.text: Food poisoning only affects people over sixty-five.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Anyone can get food poisoning, but vulnerable groups tend to be affected far more seriously.

answers.text: Extra care is only needed with raw meat, never with cooked or ready-to-eat food.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Many high-risk foods are cooked or ready to eat, such as cooked rice, gravy and prepared salads.

items.html:
A balanced diet and the Eatwell Guide

The NHS Eatwell Guide shows the balance of foods that make up a healthy diet over a day or week. It divides food into groups and shows roughly how much of each we should aim for:

  • Fruit and vegetables: aim for at least five portions a day.
  • Starchy carbohydrates: potatoes, bread, rice and pasta, choosing wholegrain where possible.
  • Protein: beans, pulses, fish, eggs and meat.
  • Dairy or alternatives: milk, cheese and yoghurt or fortified substitutes.
  • Oils and spreads: only small amounts, and limit foods high in fat, salt and sugar.

Why nutrients matter in later life

Each nutrient does a job. Protein helps maintain muscle and heal wounds, calcium and vitamin D keep bones strong, fibre supports digestion, and iron and vitamins keep the body working well. Older and unwell people often eat smaller amounts, so every mouthful needs to count.

Appetite can fade with age, illness or medication, and small, frequent, nourishing meals often work better than large plates. Remember that the Eatwell Guide is aimed at healthy adults; someone who is underweight or at risk of malnutrition may need a higher-calorie, fortified approach set out in their care plan rather than a low-fat one.

items.question: The standard Eatwell Guide balance of foods is the right target for everyone, including people who are underweight or at risk of malnutrition.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. The Eatwell Guide is designed for the general healthy population. People who are underweight or malnourished often need a higher-calorie, fortified diet set out in their care plan.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. This is false: someone who is underweight or malnourished may need a fortified, higher-calorie diet rather than the standard Eatwell balance.

items.html:
Why fluids matter so much

Every part of the body needs fluid to work properly. The NHS suggests most adults aim for around six to eight drinks a day, though the right amount varies from person to person and some may be on a fluid plan. Older people often feel thirst less strongly, so they can become dehydrated without realising it.

Poor hydration is linked to serious problems in care: urinary infections, constipation, confusion, dizziness and falls, pressure ulcers and kidney problems. Keeping people well hydrated is one of the simplest and most important things you do.

Spotting dehydration and encouraging fluids

Learn to notice the early signs so you can act before someone becomes unwell:

  • Dark, strong-smelling urine: a common early clue that fluids are low.
  • Dry mouth and lips: along with headache, tiredness or dizziness.
  • New confusion or drowsiness: which should always be reported.

Encourage drinks by offering them regularly, respecting preferred drinks, and keeping an accessible cup and jug within easy reach. Foods such as soup, jelly and fruit add fluid too. Where intake is being monitored, record drinks accurately on a fluid balance chart and report any concerns.

items.question: During a visit you notice Mr Bianchi has become unusually confused and his urine is very dark. What is the most appropriate response?

answers.text: Offer him a drink he likes, encourage fluids, record his intake and report the new confusion to a senior or nurse.
answers.feedback: Correct. These can be signs of dehydration; encouraging fluids and reporting the change promptly allows any underlying cause to be checked.

answers.text: Assume it is normal for his age and say nothing.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. New confusion is never simply ‘normal ageing’ and should always be reported.

answers.text: Restrict his drinks so he does not need the toilet as often.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Restricting fluids would make dehydration worse and could cause serious harm.

answers.text: Wait several days to see whether it settles on its own.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Delaying could allow him to become seriously unwell; the signs should be acted on now.

items.html:
The right environment and position

Mealtimes are about more than food; they are social, enjoyable and a chance for people to keep control of their day. Give meals the time they deserve rather than rushing them. A calm, pleasant setting, free of unpleasant smells and clutter, helps people relax and eat well.

Good positioning matters for comfort and safety. Support the person to sit upright and well-supported, and sit down beside them at eye level rather than standing over them. Offer real choice about what and how much they eat, and encourage independence with adapted plates or cutlery instead of taking over.

Pace, assistance and cultural needs

When you assist someone to eat, follow their lead. Go at their pace, offer small amounts, and give plenty of time to chew and swallow before the next mouthful. Alternate food with sips of drink, watch for tiredness, and use gentle verbal encouragement rather than hurrying.

Food is deeply personal and often tied to culture and faith. Someone may need halal or kosher food, follow a vegetarian or vegan diet, or observe fasting. Always respect these choices, along with personal likes and dislikes, and make sure they are recorded in the care plan so everyone honours them consistently.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on dignity at mealtimes.
items.textField: To support someone safely to eat, help them to sit *upright* and sit beside them at eye *level*. Assist at their own *pace*, offering small amounts and allowing time to chew and *swallow*. A person’s cultural and religious food needs should be respected and written in their care *plan*.

items.html:
Modified textures and fortified diets

Some people cannot manage ordinary food and drink safely. Those with swallowing difficulties (dysphagia) may need texture-modified food or thickened fluids. The IDDSI framework gives these standard levels — from thin drinks through to soft and bite-sized or pureed food — so everyone uses the same descriptions.

A speech and language therapist (SALT) assesses swallowing and sets the level a person needs. Your role is to follow that plan exactly and never change the texture or thickness yourself. Others may need a fortified diet with extra calories and protein, or a strict allergen-free diet where even a trace could cause serious harm.

A mealtime scenario

Mrs Kaur’s care plan says she is on IDDSI Level 5 minced and moist food with mildly thick (Level 2) drinks, following a SALT assessment. Today she is tired, the kitchen is busy, and a colleague suggests just giving her the normal shepherd’s pie and a cup of tea to save time.

Giving her ordinary food and thin tea would go against her prescribed plan and could cause her to choke or aspirate. The safe, respectful choice is to prepare her meal and drink to the correct levels, support her calmly and unhurried, and raise any concerns about her swallowing with the nurse or SALT rather than changing things yourself.

items.question: Mrs Kaur is prescribed minced and moist food with mildly thick drinks after a SALT assessment. A colleague suggests giving her normal food and thin tea to save time. What should you do?

answers.text: Prepare her food and drink to the prescribed IDDSI levels and raise any swallowing concerns with the nurse or SALT.
answers.feedback: Correct. The prescribed texture keeps her safe from choking and aspiration; only the SALT can review or change the level.

answers.text: Give her the normal food and tea, as she seems well enough today.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Changing her prescribed textures could cause choking or aspiration and is never a care worker’s decision to make.

answers.text: Decide yourself to thicken the tea a little more than usual to be safe.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. You should follow the level set by the SALT exactly, not adjust it up or down on your own judgement.

answers.text: Skip her meal so she does not have to eat while tired.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Missing meals risks poor nutrition and hydration; she should be supported to eat safely at her own pace.
